data.title: UnitedHealth Vs. Centene: Next Week's Q3 Earnings Should Trigger Gains For Both
data.description:
Investment Overview

If we take a look at a graph charting the performance of the U.S. five largest health insurers by market cap - UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH), CVS Health (CVS), Cigna (CI), Elevance (ELV), Humana (HUM), and Centene Corporation (NYSE:CNC) - the difference in share price performance is remarkable.

CVS Health - up 43%, has performed very strongly. Humana and Elevance - up 14% and 15% - reasonably well, while UnitedHealth (UNH) - down 35%, and Centene - down 43% - the subjects of this post, are the stragglers.

This chart only tells half the story, however. In December 2024, CVS stock traded at $44 per share, down ~55% on a two-year basis, and as recently as June this year, Humana stock traded at $220, down >50% on an 18-month basis.

In short, most U.S. health insurers have suffered major downward share price corrections in the past couple of years - even Cigna stock sank to a low of $262 per share in July this year, down nearly 30% on a nine-month basis - but some have been able to stage remarkable comebacks, making this sector an intriguing one for the opportunistic investor.

Both CVS and Humana have suffered deep crises, missing earnings targets, withdrawing guidance, and, in CVS' case, firing numerous members of their senior management team and working with activist investors to try to return to growth. Clearly, these measures appear to be working - CVS stock is up >90% year-to-date, while Humana stock has gained a more modest 16%, but >30% since July.

My working thesis is therefore that, given UnitedHealth's business is very similar to CVS', and Centene's - being a pure-play insurer - similar to Humana's, and given that both companies stock price collapses occurred more recently than CVS/Humana, perhaps we should back both to stage a similar recovery?

If that thesis is correct, then now would be a good time to buy stock in either - I recently bought stock in UnitedHealth myself.

Why UnitedHealth & Centene Both Pulled Guidance Earlier This Year

The triggers for the selloffs in the health insurer market have been broadly similar for every insurer - an unexpectedly high rise in healthcare utilization among patients. Take the Medicare Advantage ("MA") market, for example.

According to KFF.org, an independent research organization, ~54% of eligible Medicare beneficiaries are enrolled in MA plans - ~34.1m people out of 62.8m eligible. That's up from ~19% in 2007. The share is forecast to increase to ~64% by 2034, and ~70% of all enrollees have independent plans, which are open for general enrollment. UnitedHealth has 29% of all MA enrollees - ~9.9m members, with Humana second, 17%, or 5.7m enrollees, and CVS third with 12%, or ~4.1m members. Centene has ~1m members.

According to Commonwealthfund.org:

The government pays Medicare Advantage plans a set rate per person, per year (around $12,000 in 2019, not including Part D–related expenses) under what’s known as a risk-based contract. That means that each plan agrees to assume the full risk of providing all care for that inclusive amount. This payment arrangement, called capitation, is also intended to provide plans with flexibility to innovate and improve the delivery of care.

Clearly, if the health insurer pays out less for the member's annual healthcare than it receives from the government, then it is able to make a profit - this is usually measured by the health insurer's medical benefit ratio - amount spent on healthcare, divided by premiums received.

Medicare Advantage offers unique advantages that traditional Medicare doesn't offer - eyeglasses, hearing aids, and dental care, for example, and most cover Part D prescription drug benefits, and in recent years, telehealth services. It can be restrictive in other ways, for example, members only have access to "in network" doctors, and prior authorization is required before some treatments of service are authorized.

Long story short, in its earlier days, MA plans were very lucrative for insurers, but in 2024, the Centers for Medicaid and Medicare Services ("CMS") opted to increase rate payments for MA plans for 2025 by just 3.7%, which insurers interpreted as an overall reduction, given rising healthcare utilization.

The decision had a devastating effect as companies with high exposure to MA saw their medical benefit ratios rise, and were forced to adjust, and ultimately scrap guidance. UnitedHealth suspended its 2025 guidance in May this year, and also announced the resignation of its CEO. Two months later, its stock price reached a five-year low price of ~$240 per share.

In Centene's case, the company withdrew its guidance on 1st July, no doubt in part contributing to UnitedHealth's ongoing declines. In Centene's case, it was its Affordable Care Act ("ACA") marketplace coverage that was causing problems, as well as its MA business, as analysts concluded that fewer people would receive Medicaid coverage, and those that did would require increased amounts of healthcare services.

In its Q2 earnings release, Centene noted that its:

Health benefits ratio (HBR) of 93.0% for the second quarter of 2025 represents an increase from 87.6% in the comparable period in 2024.

UnitedHealth noted on its Q2 earnings call that:

We now expect a full year medical care ratio of 89.25%, plus or minus 25 basis points. This compares to the initial 86.5% midpoint we offered at the end of last year, with the increase driven by the factors discussed.

Why Both Companies Q3 Earnings Should Trigger A Bull Run On Their Stock

Centene stock reached its lowest point of 2025 in August - ~$25 per share - as did UnitedHealth - ~$237. Based on current stock prices of $34.4 and $360.5, we may note a 35% rise in Centene's share price and a >50% increase in UnitedHealth's.

Buying the dip in August when uncertainty abounded would have been risky, but with the benefit of hindsight, it was also rewarding; however, both companies share prices remain heavily discounted to former highs, and there are reasons to be optimistic ahead of Q3 earnings.

UnitedHealth reinstated its guidance when announcing its Q2 earnings - revenues of $445bn - $448bn, and adjusted earnings per share of $16. The company has been fairly tight-lipped since, and I would not necessarily bet the farm on a guidance hike; however, I would be very surprised if guidance is downgraded, as that would seriously undermine the authority of the new management team, led by CEO Stephen Hemsley, a member of the Board of Directors, who was also CEO of the company between 2006 and 2017.

United's share price collapsed because the company got its sums wrong, anticipating higher payments than it ultimately received, with lower healthcare utilization, and the company has to show it has learned from its mistakes.

Nevertheless, there is good news for UnitedHealth in that the CMS has relented, and pledged to increase rate payments for MA plans by >5% in 2026. That won't help improve performance in 2025, but ought to give it a lift in 2026. Furthermore, UnitedHealth has announced that its individual MA footprint will be reduced by 109 counties in 2026, although it will still have massive MA exposure, remaining in 2,787 counties in 2026.

In Centene's case, the company recently reaffirmed its forecast for diluted earnings per share ("EPS") in 2025 of $1.75, and during a recent fireside chat at the Deutsche Bank Healthcare Summit, told the audience that:

Underneath that, the Medicaid results for July and August were supportive of the HBR (healthcare benefit ratio) improvement trajectory that we're looking for in the back half of the year. So pleased with that. We've obviously been very focused on the refiling process for 2026 Marketplace rates. As of today, we've been able to successfully refile in states covering 95% of our membership.

The key thing to note in relation to both Centene and UnitedHealth is that it is not revenues that are important, but profits. In 2024, Centene reported earnings per share ("EPS") of $6.33, a figure which looks set to decline by >70% in 2025, but I expect Q3 to be the signal that improvement is coming, and quickly. Management has advised that its MA division will not reach breakeven until 2027, but clearly, Medicaid margins are already on an uptrend.

In United's case, EPS - on an adjusted basis at least - will decline in 2025, but on its Q2 earnings call, CEO Hemsley told analysts:

Looking to 2026, at this distance, I would expect solid but moderate earnings growth. As we look further ahead, we see our earnings growth outlook strengthening quickly in 2027 and pacing steadily upward over the succeeding years.

Concluding Analysis: Both UnitedHealth & Centene Can Put An End To Crisis Talk With Positive Q3 Earnings - I Expect Nothing Less

Rising healthcare utilization did not just blindside health insurers in 2024 and 2025; it also blindsided the government, also, which is ultimately responsible for footing the bill for health insurance through the CMS and through Medicaid, and Medicare.

Arguably, however, it's been the health insurers that have borne the brunt of the backlash, as their profit margins - never especially high, despite the hundreds of billions of revenues taken in each year - have been squeezed, and their entire business models have been put under serious strain.

I believe the CMS' commitment to raising rate payments in 2026 for MA is a tacit acknowledgement that it, too, got its sums wrong in terms of how much it should pay insurers to administer healthcare, and going forward, I'd also expect insurers to ask plan members to pay a higher premium, in exchange for better quality of service. In terms of Medicaid, Centene's CFO had this to say during the company's recent fireside chat.

Medicaid is a tough business. It's hand-to-hand combat in terms of convincing your partner through data and what the run rate is, but feel confident that we're still on track to improve Medicaid going into 2026 from a margin standpoint off of our 2025 guidance.

UnitedHealth has ~400k employees, and Centene, ~60k. These companies must be incentivized by the possibility of making an annual profit, or their businesses collapse. The government and CMS is not being asked to fund a 2008 financial crisis-style bailout of the healthcare industry, but the industry does need to be stabilized and recalibrated, and I suspect there is agreement on all sides in that respect.

Because of this, I'm very doubtful that either Centene or UnitedHealth will lurch back into crisis on the back of Q3 earnings, and while I don't think either company will radically hike its full-year 2025 guidance when they report earnings next week, I do believe they will sound a more positive note on 2026 and beyond, and that, in turn, will allow them to complete the same kind of share price recovery we have seen with CVS Health, and to a lesser extent Humana, in 2025.

All things considered, then, my sentiment on both companies is bullish ahead of Q3 earnings (UNH reports pre-market on the 28th, and CNC pre-market on the 29th), and I would point to an additional positive. I expect both companies to report that the percentage of its MA plan members in plans rated four stars or higher (which qualify for additional bonuses) has increased, aiding profitability.

If I had to choose one to back over the other, I'd point to the dividends paid by UnitedHealth, which currently yields 2.45% per annum, adding additional insurance in the unlikely event of share price losses.

data.title: DroneShield: Fundamentals Don't Support A Record Rally
data.description:
As drone usage expands across global militaries, DroneShield Limited (OTCPK:DRSHF) has quietly emerged as an innovative player. Most investors have been busy focusing on companies with the best drone technology but regardless of who makes the best drones, there is only one listed company that offers counterdrone technologies, and that’s DRSHF. The company uses radar, radio frequency, and sensing systems powered by AI to detect, track, and neutralize drone threats.

I have looked at the company in detail and find their products, business model, and upcoming opportunities quite attractive. However, the execution, especially on the financial front leaves a lot to be desired. The stock has rallied despite this, and I will look at why the current stock levels make the stock an unattractive option for investors.

Counter Drone Technologies

DroneShield Limited sells both hardware and software for drone detection and operates in the counter-drone niche. It is the only listed company in the world that is exclusively working on counter-UAS (unmanned aerial systems) technologies. It is a global company operating in 70+ countries, with most of its revenue coming from Europe. It makes handheld and wearables solutions like the DroneGun Mk4 and also vehicular solutions like the DroneSentry-X Mk2. These are well complemented by fixed-site solutions and software that help complete the counter-drone ecosystem.

DroneSentry-X Mk2: The Sales Engine

The company’s flagship product in the DroneSentry-X Mk2. It combines RF sensing, advanced radar, and infrared technology into a single modular platform. With the help of AI powered SensorFusion system, real-time detection and identification of drones is made possible. It can be deployed on a short notice and used for both fixed-site and mobile operations. What makes it so attractive is its ability to both detect and defeat. However, it must be pointed out that defeat does not mean it physically shoots down drones. The Mk2 renders oncoming drones harmless through electronic countermeasures rather than kinetic weapons. This includes things like jamming, spoofing GPS, or performing a protocol takeover.

Commercially, the DroneSentry-X Mk2 provides the hardware anchor that the company builds its ecosystem around. So every sale of the product not only brings upfront revenue but also the potential for recurring software subscriptions.

Software as a Service (SaaS)

This brings me to the company’s software prospects. As it perfects its hardware and deploys it among an increasing number of customers, their dependence on the accompanying software will increase. These subscription-only services could eventually overshadow the hardware revenue. Right now, SaaS accounts for only 4% of the company’s total revenues, despite a 400% growth compared to the same period in 2024. This fast-growing segment is only going to get stronger as the company sells more hardware.

This segment is driven by the DroneSentry-C2. The above-mentioned DroneSentry-X Mk2 synchronizes well with the DroneSentry-C2, a command and control software. Customers who use the DroneSentry-X Mk2 end up also buying the DroneSentry-C2 software, helping bring customers into the DroneShield ecosystem. This results in recurring, reliable revenue.

The Drone Wall Initiative

The Drone Wall initiative by the European Union is a comprehensive counter-drone system intended to protect European airspace from Russian incursions. Systems installed under this initiative are expected to be operational by 2027. Since DRSHF already has a strong presence in Europe and any counter-drone technology will need to work well alongside existing infrastructure, this initiative is expected to add to its commercial prospects. The company’s end-to-end solutions that also integrate AI could help accelerate a project that is already expected to be rapidly executed, especially after the recent incursion of 21 Russian drones into Poland.

Having said that, the importance of such a counter-drone system needs to be realized. If the future will be all about drone warfare, NATO would probably want to work with a contractor that is deeply integrated within its defense systems. Considering the pace of the deployment of the Drone Wall, one also wonders if the company can keep up with the manufacturing requirements. The existing R&D and manufacturing capacity expansion plans look good, but not good enough to scale a defense program that would lay the foundations of decades of future technological development.

3Q 2025 Was Truly Impressive!!!

DRSHF announced the third quarter results today, showcasing an impressive turnaround. The quarterly topline figure of $93 million is almost 11x higher compared to the same quarter last year. In fact, the third quarter sales are up 161% compared to $35.6 million sales recorded during the entire 2024. These figures clearly represent an astounding uplift in global demand for counter-drone capabilities. Being the only publicly traded counter-drone company in the world, the management emphasized exploring opportunities within a $60 billion global addressable market for counter-drones, including $30 billion military market. The management also highlighted its ambitions to move towards more recurring revenue streams through SaaS, which currently accounts for only 5% of the topline. During the quarter, SaaS revenues stood at $3.5 million, a 400% jump compared to same period last year, and the management aims to generate between 30% to 40% of the business from SaaS within the next 5 years. In fact, every product launch from this point onwards is expected to carry at least a single SaaS component, including the offerings for civilian market. For instance, the company launched SentryCiv back in August, which is a subscription-based counter-drone solution for infrastructure protection.

Moving on, the business continues to operate at average gross margins of 65%, which is an adjusted figure after accounting for some lower-margin third-party products that the company sells. These include equipment such as cameras and radars that offer gross margins of around 15% to 20%. Hence, an average of 65% including these offerings, makes up for a very lucrative case. On the operational side, there is one critical factor that is both an advantage and a disadvantage at the same time. So the company currently incurs circa $100 million in fixed cash costs every year. This fixed cost structure was the primary detriment to DRSHF’s profitability in recent years. But on the flip side it also offers operational leverage to the business, which can benefit from an anticipated sales growth in the future. This means that now those higher sales figures will turn into magnified operating profits. Earlier, I spoke about the management’s ambitions to increase the share of recurring SaaS-based revenues in the mix. Connect the dots, and you will be able to see how financials will pan out in the future.

During the third quarter, DRSHF also exhibited a very strong growth in cash receipts which were recorded at $77.4 million. This resulted in a massive 751% jump compared to $9.1 million receipts during the same period in 2024. All the above paved the way for a drastic change in operating cash flows. As highlighted already, a higher level of operating leverage and record revenue growth led to operating cash flows of $20 million. For reference, during the third quarter in 2024, the operating cash flows were -$19 million, and apart from 2023 the operating cash flows have remained in negative territory since 2015.

What Does The Last Quarter Hold?

The management holds a very optimistic view for the last quarter, having already secured $193 million in revenues for 2025. They aim to remain profitable on a consistent basis and keep their operating cash flows positive. They anticipate rising procurement within the counter-drone market, which will be geographically agnostic. For the final quarter, management forecasts a best-case scenario of $130 million in revenues. This forecast comes at the back of $27 million worth of finished inventory that could generate around $80 million in sales. What is noteworthy about this best-case scenario is that the management deemed these $130 million of revenues as profitable revenues. Given a growing business pipeline of around $2.5 billion, these estimates do not appear to be unrealistic. However, as an investor, one should always take caution when it comes to a business that is going through a turnaround.

A Historical Perspective

Having appreciated the performance in the third quarter, it is also important to look at the historical performance of the business to get a more holistic view of things. Undoubtedly, DRSHF kept expanding its topline over the years in a very aggressive manner. Annual sales for 2024 were $35.6 million, slightly lower than $36.9 million in 2023 but monumental compared to $4.3 million in 2020. This translates into a CAGR of 70% between 2020 and 2024, which is an obvious sign of a thriving demand for a specific niche.

But the above-mentioned revenue figures were not enough to make up for high fixed operational cost component of the business, which was discussed earlier in the article. A cost base comprised of SG&A, R&D and other operating costs led to continued operational losses as shown in the figure above. What kept things manageable was the fact that the business was not financially leveraged, hence there weren’t any alarming interest costs to deal with. Moreover, the company made some smart investments worth around $98 million during 2023 and 2024, which generated a decent amount of non-operational income to limit the net losses. However, these factors had nothing to do with the core operations, which was the real pain point. A high cost base is something that will persist and pose a risk to the company’s profitability, and the only way around that is to expand the topline. We did see a notable jump in the third quarter sales and the management does seem very optimistic about the coming periods too. But for investors it is better to wait until things reflect more consistency.

Stock Performance

DRSHF stock is up 542% this year despite being down 27% so far this month. It is a volatile stock and considering its unique niche, it is not surprising that it has enjoyed a great rally in the ongoing drone warfare race.

The stock is closing in on the 50-day moving average, and considering the fact that this level is also a previous resistance, one can expect some sideways movement in the short term. Such extreme gains and price swings are common among stocks associated with drone warfare. As great an opportunity as this industry presents, it also comes with volatility and downside risk. I have looked at three other stocks that are not necessarily peers of DRSHF but do operate in the same niche: Ondas Holdings Inc. (ONDS), Mercury Systems, Inc. (MRCY), and Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT).

All these companies have experienced extraordinary volatility. ONDS has gained 720% in just 6 months, with MRCY and RCAT more than doubling and tripling in a year, respectively. DRSHF, however, is more stable than the other when it comes to profitability. The company has gross margins of 67.62% though its operating margins are negative. ONDS, MRCY, and RCAT are not profitable either. DRSHF is also trading at a Price/Sales of 33.71, more than three times higher than its 5-year average.

Risks

One factor that restricts the expansion of the non-governmental side of the business is regulation. Customers like airports, prisons, oil & gas factories, electricity grids, and other critical civilian installations can use counterdrone measures. However, it can be hard to allow civilian entities to shoot down drones on their own. The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) prohibits anyone except federal agencies from jamming drones. Similarly in Europe, the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) allows detection, but not any electronic countermeasures. This means either the government has to take oversight over the use of anti-drone equipment or simply not allow its civilian use, hurting the company’s prospects.

Dilution risk is also a concern for potential investors. As is the case with most businesses undergoing operational and financial struggles, DRSHF had to rely on external financing to cover its operating and capital expenditures. The company decided to turn to its shareholders for raising the required capital by executing a Share Purchase Plan (SPP) in 2024, generating $15 million proceeds in exchange for issuing new shares. The offer was aimed at raising capital from eligible shareholders who wished to buy shares at an almost 19% discount to the prevailing market price. However, any existing shareholder who does not wish to do so faces dilution risk in such a situation. The company intends to keep the third-quarter momentum going by remaining profitable and keeping operating cash flows positive. For now, the company's reported cash reserves of A$235.2 million (circa $153 million) provide good enough cushion to meet any capital needs in the short term. Hence, for now the risk does get mitigated to a large extent. However, any deviations in the future could create the need to raise more capital through more offerings, which poses additional dilution risk for investors. Beyond the capital needs, the company also has an employee incentive scheme that involves issuing unquoted securities like options. In early 2024, the company issued an additional 700,000 shares in that regard, amid vesting of performance options. Going forward more shares issues can be expected which also exposes investors to dilution risk.

Despite its upside, the risks associated with the company’s fixed cost base cannot be ignored. High operating leverage does create an opportunity to convert sales into larger profits, but any hiccups could also lead to substantial downside. Managing a healthy topline growth in the future now seems to have become more of a compulsion.

Finally, the stretched valuations will not go unnoticed by market participants. The third-quarter announcements might create a short-term interest in the stock, but at some point, the investors will start viewing it as an overbought stock. Therefore, one should be fully prepared for a major sell-off at some point, bringing the share to a more realistic valuation range.

Conclusion

Recent quarter results do give a reason for being bullish on DRSHF. But the valuation metrics shown above overshadow any such sentiments, as the stock is clearly overvalued. Despite favorable projections by the management, it is hard to overlook the historical numbers where the business kept struggling with high operating costs. Next couple of quarters will be crucial to understanding the long-term business prospects, and investors must observe things closely before taking any positions. Keeping in mind certain risks discussed in the previous section, I will give a Sell rating for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: September CPI Contained A Hidden Surprise
data.description:
As mentioned in "Shutdown or Not, Inflation Will Print," the Bureau of Labor Statistics ("BLS") released the consumer price index ("CPI") this month despite not releasing any other statistics due to the government shutdown. This one was deemed essential because it is used to index cost-of-living adjustments for Social Security.

The report came in cool, at 3% for both core and non-core CPI, while the consensus was 3.1% for both.

For reference, here's my prediction from that article:

If the CPI print comes in at 3% or below, I will expect the market to rally on the news. The market is already pricing in the possibility of a high CPI and lower rates...

Lo and behold, a cooler print gets us exactly that, markets up and bonds flat.

Let's take a deeper look at why, because it's about more than just the number. It's also about where the inflation was within the report.

September CPI Report

The "hidden surprise" I alluded to was the sudden thrust of energy prices, in which gasoline inflation hit 4.1%, bucking its deflationary trend that had been keeping CPI suppressed relative to Core CPI, which excludes food and fuel. With this rise, the numbers have converged.

That was a stark change in the data for gasoline (XB1:COM), as gasoline has been deflating on and off for a while, now suddenly doubling its rise from any previous month in the report.

Interestingly enough, this reversal doesn't extend to other energy categories; energy services like electricity and gas utilities deflated month-to-month, but are up in their YoY figures. Gasoline is still notably negative YoY.

Rising gasoline prices may appear harmful to consumers at first glance, because the higher gas prices are, the less folks tend to drive when it comes to discretionary miles (they still drive to work no matter how much gas costs, turns out), which impacts other factors of the economy, like how much they consume. However, rising gasoline prices are usually in response to a rising demand for gas, which is a positive note for the economy.

If all energy prices were rising at the same time, I would expect this to be an indicator of economic pain. Just gasoline? Much more positive from my view.

Of course, this is a short-term view, as the YoY figure for gas utilities is over 11%. That's a huge jump, and reflects the longer-term trend. What I am most interested in is the future, not the past, so seeing these inflection points when they come up is important — it's one thing to watch gas rise all year, and it's another to spot when it pivots.

Is This a Pivot Point?

I use that term "pivot," but I need to make clear that I am just discussing energy prices. The pivot from gasoline actually caused energy to be a net contributor to inflation this month, as opposed to previous months this year when it was deflationary.

CPI is still not looking so hot, with goods inflation continuing to creep up. Goods inflation continuing to rise steadily, increasingly contributing to overall inflation, which is still dominated by services, as per usual.

My current theory about goods inflation is that many large retailers are sitting on tariff costs and "eating them," but are slowly unwinding them out to consumers; the frogs don't notice when you slowly raise the temperature. They only realize when it's too late.

If the courts rule that some tariffs are illegal, we should see a reversal in these rising goods costs, or at least a leveling out. For now, the data looks indicative of goods sellers protecting profit margins without scaring away customers.

The Fed Will Cut Rates

Most of the eyes on the CPI report cared about one thing: what will it prompt the Fed to do?

A hot report could have caused a pause to our current rate path. While the Fed seems more inclined to tackle the labor market than inflation right now, it stands to reason that if inflation began to run hotter than expectations, they may need to change their focus.

It's very likely that we are going to see another 25bp cut at the end of the month, which has been the expectation for some time now. The futures market is pricing in a 95% chance of this happening, and I saw the only barrier between that cut and now being this CPI report.

CPI coming in under expectations is perfect cover to continue the march toward the neutral rate. I still expect us to fall 25bp now and another 25bp in December. So long as current labor trends hold up, it's looking like a rising CPI won't stop it.

Conclusion

Ultimately, this CPI report was a continuation of the inflationary trend we've been seeing over the past few months, but it came in slightly cooler than expected. The hidden surprise was a sudden jump in gasoline prices, which may actually be a positive sign for the economy given that it is alone in the fuels category, and other fuels have stayed relatively put or even fallen this month.

This strength gives enough cover for the Fed to continue lowering rates from here, especially since their focus seems squarely on the labor market right now. Rising inflation isn't as much of a worry if wages keep pace. The Fed knows this, and they are acting accordingly. This balancing act is tough to watch, because I don't want to see them fail. For now, it's looking like they'll make it cleanly across.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: My 2025 AI Playbook: Bubble Or Supercycle?
data.description:
The AI 2025 paradox

As a crowd, people keep calling it a "bubble" but after a doubling of the market, historical research shows that the probability of another doubling beats that of a -50% decline. At the same time, hyperscalers are pushing an unfathomable $400 billion capital expenditure cycle, which is mostly financed by cash rather than by debt. Is it an irrational decision? Maybe. Can it be invested in? Yes, definitely, but with risk control. Well, I keep my position in the theme, I decrease the beta, and I only make a purchase when the figures justify the risk.

Where do we start (in two lines)

In my two recent stock market analyses, I explained what I actually do in the portfolios I manage and in my own personal portfolio. I assigned the overall market a tactical "sell" rating due to high valuations. I believe a healthy correction is needed (almost inevitable). We require oxygen. I discussed market sensitivity not in a macroeconomic or fundamental sense but rather in structural and technical terms. I won't repeat everything here, as it would make for too long an article, so anyone who wants to catch up can read “Don't Buy This Dip: Why This Time Really Is Different” and “Not A Bubble (Yet), But One Spark Can Torch This Market.”

This work starts exactly from here: it's a clear inquiry continuing the argument. Let's try to answer these questions: What is a bubble? At what point can it break, and how can we address it?

I hope you enjoy reading it, and as usual, please let me know what you think in the comments section. Let's make the most of this platform to share factual opinions, helpful data, and perspectives that allow us to better navigate the financial markets.

Why I am selling on the market today

Even though I have given a “sell” rating, I remain constructive about the market in the long term. I have always been and always will be (otherwise I'd have to think about changing jobs!). Joking aside, even flies know that staying invested pays off much more than trying to time the market. But when everything is moving too fast, when euphoria rises and valuations start to live on another planet, you also need to have the courage to slow down. Reducing is not being negative. It is being clear-headed.

If someone told me today that they wanted to put all their savings into the markets, I would obviously tell them to do so, but I would also tell them not to jump in with both feet but to enter gradually to dilute the risk, especially when valuations are so high.

If, on the other hand, someone told me that they had to invest everything immediately, then I would encourage them to think a little more deeply about it, such as diversifying their portfolio both in terms of stocks and alternative assets to stocks, such as bonds, precious metals, etc. In short, balancing and mitigating the risk of the portfolio.

From some of the comments I have read on my previous articles, I gather that many people agree with this view, even if a sort of pessimistic tone seems to prevail. That was not my intention, but perhaps it is better that way. All in all, it is better to be pessimistic and cautious than overly optimistic and reckless, because by doing so we may have saved a few lives, financially speaking.

After a doubling of the markets, the probability of another doubling is higher than that of a collapse

As an investor, I live with an unshakeable belief in two things:

  1. 30 years from now, my assets invested today will have grown significantly
  2. that in the next 30 years there will be crises, anxieties, concerns, recessions, inflation, wars, and pandemics.

Investing is a predominantly unpleasant experience and pays off (more than other assets) precisely because you have to endure constant suffering.

The point is that right now everything feels like a bubble. “If the newspapers say so, then it must be true.” But remember that fear sells. And the more the market rises, the more it will seem like the definitive sign of an imminent financial catastrophe. Of course, that will happen too; it cannot be ruled out. Consider that statistically, from 1929 to today, there have been an average of 13 bear markets, one every 6-7 years, with an average decline of 40.6%, and in the worst cases, even more than -50%. So I would not be surprised if in the next 30 years, we see at least five, and one of them probably deep.

But what no one talks about is that in the five years following each bear market, the market has rebounded by an average of +147%. Here is a table highlighting the main market crises with their corresponding bear markets (-20% from their highs), dates, peaks, market lows, and various price changes. It may be useful in this context.

This is not to imply that a 40% crisis will occur in two to four years, but only to highlight the fact that such declines are part of the game and that the recovery is remarkable. In 2009, the late Charlie Munger, Warren Buffett's lifelong partner, said:

You can argue that if you're not willing to react with equanimity to a market price decline of 50% two or three times a century, you're not fit to be a common shareholder, and you deserve the mediocre result you're going to get.

This is what investing in stocks is all about: high expected returns, because there is a lot of suffering to endure, theoretically temporary, even if no one knows “how long it is temporary” or whether the next market apocalypse will begin in 2025, 2026, or 2036. Bubbles happen much more often than we think. But the fact that everything seems like a bubble today (and that even names like Zuckerberg, Altman, and Bezos admit it) does not mean that it really is. In fact, if we look at the data, it is statistically unlikely (not impossible) that the one we are currently immersed in is a real bubble. Allow me to explain myself better with the help of William Goetzmann, a professor at the Yale School of Management, who wrote an article in the WSJ two months ago on the subject of bubbles, which I believe is very relevant today.

His goal was to distinguish between investors' subjective opinions about imminent (or presumed) bubbles and the actual behavior of the markets. Instead of joining the chorus of those shouting “We're in a bubble!”—as newspapers and media outlets now do on a daily basis to get a few more clicks and sell more copies—he did something different: he tried to understand, based on data, how often markets that appear to be in a bubble actually are.

The result? Less than we think, much less. Analyzing over a century of data on dozens of countries, he found that real bubbles, i.e., those cases where a boom actually ends in a crash, are extremely rare events, less than 1%. And what struck me most is that after a doubling of the markets (100%), the probability of another doubling is higher than that of a crash. So there's no point in sitting around hoping that the world is about to collapse. Sorry, but it happens much less often than you might think.

Flashback or Déjà vu

From this perspective, the dot-com bubble that burst in early 2000 was almost a textbook example. The S&P 500 peaked on March 23, 2000, at 1,532 points. Three years earlier, on April 8, 1997, it stood at 766. In less than three years, the market had doubled. On October 9, 2002, it bottomed out at 776 points, a drop of about 50%. The final collapse came after the Great Financial Crisis, when the market plummeted to an intraday low of 666 points on March 16, 2009. The question everyone is asking is, is the same thing happening today? I will answer definitively for everyone: “No (or at least not yet)”. As I write, the S&P is at 6,722 points. The last time it was at half its value (3,365 points) was on August 11, 2020, so it took more than five years to double. That's a long time, a very long time indeed. However, we are talking about an average annual return (including dividends) of over 13%. Extraordinary, but I don't think it's bubble territory. And to put it into perspective, if the S&P closes at 7,000 points this year, it would be +19% YTD (+20% if we include dividends, approximately). From January 2021 to December 2025, that would mean about +90% in five years, while by comparison, during the dot-com bubble, from 1995 to 1999, the S&P gained +247%. That was a bubble pace, a whole different league. Another story. And that gives you an idea of the level of exaggeration in those years.

On a technical and fundamental level, I have created this table, and in my analysis a few days ago, I also explained how to interpret it in practical terms. I recommend reading it.

Emotional impact on the average investor

One very interesting thing is that we humans tend to filter information selectively, giving greater resonance to information that has a greater emotional impact. Looking at data from 1900 to the present, the probability of a financial bubble bursting in any “5-year window” is just 0.5%. And this is one of those classic situations where our perception tends to greatly overestimate the probability of an adverse event. Consider that periodic surveys of investors and managers have shown that they expected a catastrophic crisis in the months following each survey with an average probability of 10-20%. The latest survey even reports 33%.

Let me repeat that to be clear. Statistically, bubbles have a 0.5% chance of occurring in any 5-year period, while the average investor expects up to a one in five chance of experiencing a bubble in the next 6 months. Crazy. It's crazy how we tend to overestimate the probability of extreme events just because they have a greater impact on our imagination. This is a perfect example of what in psychology is called base rate neglect, i.e., we tend to ignore the actual historical frequencies of rare events and replace them with our emotional perceptions. Perhaps the scars of the dot-com bubble (before) and the 2008 crisis (after) have left their mark. Many investors today still live with that fear. The really interesting part of the study, however, is something else: “What usually happens in the five years after a market doubles in value?” Well, only in 15% of cases does a 50% crash occur. In 26% of cases, on the other hand, the market doubles again. Everything else? Intermediate movements, up and down, but no disasters.

So, after a boom, it is almost twice as likely that another one will follow, rather than a bubble bursting, and the fact that an index has just doubled does not make a collapse any more likely. The paradox is entirely psychological. The more the market rises, the more we worry that it might crash. It's that strange feeling that even when everything is going well, we remain afraid that it will end badly. Finance is sadistic: it keeps us awake at night during crashes and rallies alike.

Can we stop calling it a bubble?!

I swear I'll cancel my online newspaper subscriptions. Not a day goes by without an article instilling anxiety in investors about the prospect of an overvalued stock market, unsustainable public debt, the latent threat of private credit, the gold bubble, and so on. Financial media outlets sell by fanning the flames of alleged bad news, but when things actually happen, no one cares. It's October 2025, and I'm still waiting for the famous recession that was practically certain and announced at the end of 2022. I'm waiting. I'm in no hurry. Give me a shout when it arrives!

WSJ:

Reuters:

and others..

What CEOs are saying: “industrial bubble” (and why it's different)

Industrial bubble

Speaking at Tech Week in Turin, Jeff Bezos said that this could be an “industrial bubble,” not a financial one. Stratospheric investments in data centers, for example, to support the computational capacity of large language models, are at serious risk of getting out of control, as was the case with the railway boom in the early 1900s or the millions of kilometers of network infrastructure in the 1990s.

Long-term benefits

When the bubbles burst, investors lost billions of dollars, but the infrastructure remained and, in the long term, brought a net gain in terms of innovation and, above all, wealth creation. Today's big internet winners would not exist without the bubble of the 1990s. In the short term, innovations tend to disappoint because they fuel excessive expectations, but in the long term they tend to surprise positively because they ultimately reveal their full hidden potential. It may be a bubble, but that does not mean it is an end in itself or that it will not bring long-term benefits.

Industrial bubble vs. financial bubble

In any case, we are talking about two different things. One thing is the “financial bubble,” as was the Great Financial Crisis, and another thing is the “industrial bubble.” The financial bubble was a gigantic house of cards that blew away as soon as the wind picked up, with devastating effects on the real economy, global debt, and probably also on the increase in social inequality, especially in the United States. Industrial bubbles, on the other hand, encourage innovation and can be a negative factor because they “get out of hand,” certainly, but in the long run they lead to benefits.

The elephant in the room: AI Capex 2025

Finally, and this is no small matter, in 1999 there was enormous recourse to leverage and debt to finance projects related to the “internet economy” (as it was called at the time). Today, however, the AI giants could spend over $400 billion (roughly Denmark's GDP) this year on capital expenditure, largely to finance AI projects, using their own money.

All are financed by profits and consolidated businesses that generate cash flow relatively consistently. No debt, no special leverage, no financial risk. Is there a bubble? Even if it bursts, at least there will be no damage to the entire economy, and the big tech companies will be the ones to lose out, with their own money. Of course, if things go wrong, investors will also be affected, given that we are holding their shares, but the fact that these projects are not financed by debt reduces systemic risks.

Final thoughts

Before concluding the analysis, I wanted to make some final considerations at a strictly operational level, as I usually do in my analyses. Even though markets tend to grow in the long run, portfolio risk must be adjusted based on market valuations and the macroeconomic environment. I repeat: adjust, don't run away. Mitigate short-term risks as best you can; don't liquidate everything. I want to clarify why—as someone noted in the comments below my latest articles—I “had the courage” to cut exposure decisively. But be careful: I am a flexible thematic equity fund manager, and it is my job to anticipate and protect when I see signs of technical fragility.

You may be a “buy and hold” investor, in which case you should not be afraid to remain invested. They are two different games, and there is nothing wrong with either approach. Furthermore, I would point out that diversifying the sources of risk in your portfolio does not necessarily maximize your expected return; in fact, it probably does the exact opposite. But if risk control is more important to an investor than absolute return, then diversifying to reduce the dispersion of possible future outcomes is a very good thing, especially at this point in history. Therefore, I invite you to diversify geographically, use bonds (including inflation-indexed bonds, for example), add gold to your portfolio (I recommend reading my analysis: GLD: Central Banks Are Dumping Dollars And Hoarding Gold—Strong Buy), etc., because it certainly improves the expected risk-adjusted return (not the absolute return).

What I am doing in operational terms

In the meantime, what should you do with your portfolios? Personally, I am not moving away from AI but rather managing it. I only hold assets that pay me for the risk and only choose companies with positive FCF, net cash balances, and real pricing power. Alongside this, I am building a cushion that allows me to sleep peacefully, with short-term IG bonds and 20% gold as an energy/geopolitical hedge (I explain why in the piece on GLD). I have exited themes with overly high valuations (“AI-adjacent”) that live off the capex of others with negative FCF, because if the cycle slows down, these are the first to suffer.

I am gradually re-entering only in two specific cases:

  • -8% / -12% from recent highs (I buy weakness), or
  • After quarterly results and with markets recovering, and only if a) Capex/FCF ≤ 1 with visibility (investments are covered by the cash the company generates, not by other leverage); b) Operating margin increasing despite capex rising (company investing, but margins not being squeezed); c) ROIC ≥ WACC accelerating (invested capital generating value and increasingly so).

I hedge against equity risk only when necessary, using futures if VIX > 22/25. I do not buy protection when the sea is calm, as in my opinion it costs too much and is unnecessary. If CAPEX remains structurally above FCF, multiples could compress, so I quickly and promptly shift to those who monetize immediately (services, energy, retrofits) and raise defensive stocks.

If we see delays of any kind in the AI supply chain (HBM, packaging, power delivery), revenues could slow down, so I lighten the load on the most leveraged players in the supply chain.

And you? What do you do?

Please let me know in the comments.

data.title: Barrick: Buying The Dips, Expecting New All-Time High Within A Year
data.description:
Investment thesis: Barrick Mining's (NYSE:B) share price is up over 100% YTD, even after the recent pullback. Such a significant rally will naturally lead to investor temptation to start taking some profits, which I did just recently. However, I ended up buying back a similar volume of shares on the recent pullback in its stock price, and I intend to keep adding if the pullback continues. For the longer term, I expect Barrick's share price to surpass its previous all-time high, reached in 2011, within a year and continue to rise from there.

Barrick is experiencing a significant rise in profits on the back of surging gold prices

Given the massive surge in gold prices, it should not come as a surprise that Barrick is experiencing a sizable improvement in its financial results.

As we can see, adjusted net earnings increased significantly, both in terms of Y-O-Y and compared with the previous quarter. In terms of its profitability, in the second quarter of 2024, adjusted earnings came in at 17.6% of revenues. In the latest quarter, it was 21.7%. It is an improving trend that I expect we will continue to see in the coming quarters. A highlight of note that Barrick saw as important to advertise is the earnings per share of $.47, which was the best since 2013. Its all-time peak in share price occurred two years before that at over $54/share.

As we can see, we are still very far from Barrick's all-time share price high, despite gold prices being more than double currently compared with the highs of $1,800/ounce reached in 2011.

With a forward P/E ratio of under 15, it is trading well below other mining stocks, where it is assumed that the mined mineral in question is in a sustained rally situation for the foreseeable future. This suggests that the market is still very unsure what to make of the gold price rally we saw in the last decade, with the price quadrupling since 2015. Cameco (CCJ), for instance, trades at a forward P/E ratio of around 75 currently, on expectations that uranium prices will continue to rise, with the market pricing it in. The fact that Barrick is not similarly priced suggests that the market expects a sizable and perhaps lasting pullback in gold prices, or at least it sees a very high risk of such a pullback.

It remains to be seen whether a sizable and sustained pullback in gold prices is imminent or not, but as of right now, Barrick is enjoying a period of improving financial health. This allows it to buy back shares, as it did in the last quarter, with $268 million allocated for this purpose. It is somewhat surprising that it is not also paying down debt.

It now has more cash than debt, as we can see, thanks to a significant improvement in its cash position. Gold prices may fluctuate going forward, with significant pullbacks possible. However, at current approximate gold prices, Barrick will continue to improve its financial position on the back of continued profits, and it will likely also reward shareholders with share buybacks, and potentially it might also raise its dividend.

Barrick's long-term production outlook

The last time I covered Barrick, I highlighted not only my positive outlook on gold but also its mined output growth prospects, not so much driven by gold but by copper.

I will not go into too much detail on Barrick's various projects. I just want to highlight the Reko Diq project, which is set to add to its gold mining capacity, but more importantly, it will also significantly alter its mined product composition, with copper set to play a significantly more important role than it does currently.

As we can see, the site has about 26 million ounces of gold, half of which is attributable to Barrick. The value of that gold at current market prices is about $52 billion. The 7.5 million tonnes of copper contained in the ore that is attributable to Barrick is worth about $75 billion, given the current price of copper at around $10,000/tonne.

In the second quarter of this year, Barrick sold 54,000 tonnes of copper, with a realized price of $4.36/lb, or about $9,800/tonne. Of Barrick's total revenues of almost $3.7 billion, about $500 million came from copper sales, or about 1/7 of its revenues. With higher copper production in the future, it is reasonable to expect that a higher proportion of its revenues will come from copper. It is impossible to get an approximate estimate of future revenue ratios between the two metals that it produces, given that much depends on market prices for both gold and copper. Because copper is an industrial metal and it does not trade in correlation with gold, this can offer Barrick an extra pillar of financial stability; in other words, if a dramatic decline in gold prices were to happen, it would help to cushion the blow to its financial results.

Gold & copper market outlook

In the shorter term, there is much uncertainty on the outlook for gold, given that there are no predictable ways to forecast demand, unlike industrial commodities such as copper. Major holders of gold, such as central banks, could suddenly turn and start selling after years of being net buyers. Wild price swings are therefore to be expected. In the short term. Certain fundamentals do kick in for the longer term, and in my view, the fundamentals that led to a decade-long rally in gold prices are still intact.

  • The long-term gold price rally continues to be supported by fiscal imbalances, coupled with a few other factors.

As I pointed out in a recent article, the IMF is warning that by the end of the decade, global public sector debt is set to reach 100% of GDP. A brief look at most major economies and their odds of reducing their ballooning deficits suggests that the only option left is to allow for higher inflation to achieve higher nominal growth and then try to keep spending growth under the pace of nominal economic growth achieved.

We see this trend being adopted by central banks, ranging from the Bank of England to the US Federal Reserve, where their 2% inflation target is being ignored in favor of cutting interest rates to stimulate the economy, as well as trying to reduce the fiscal pressures of higher interest rates for their indebted governments. The net effect on fiat currencies is that we are set to lose their viability as a wealth preservation mechanism, meaning that stock markets, as well as tangible assets such as gold or silver, are likely to perform well going forward when measured in fiat currency terms. The way I see it, there is no practical fundamental limit to how high gold prices may rise in the coming years, which is a concept I highlighted in an article on the GLD ETF (GLD).

  • The outlook for copper is somewhat murky.

Copper is an industrial metal with particularly high rates of consumption in the construction industry and increasingly in the electrification of the economy. EVs require up to four times more copper, or about 80 kg/vehicle, in their systems compared with a conventional car. The fact that most of the world's economic expansion comes from the developing world, where places like India, Indonesia & Vietnam are in the process of building out infrastructure, is also positive for copper's long-term outlook.

Some of the negative aspects include a slowing global economy, which does not bode well for most industrial metals. The China infrastructure demand story still seems to have legs. Some factors suggest that there is a risk that it is running out of steam, with a real estate overhang suggesting that there is no need for much more infrastructure building. Looking at both the positive as well as negative factors, the positives probably outweigh the negatives, which means that global copper demand growth will continue for the foreseeable future. Institutional forecasts are mostly bullish on long-term copper demand, but it remains to be seen.

Investment implications

I recently sold a small portion of my Barrick position, and I bought back shares this week on the significant pullback in gold prices that resulted in a roughly 10% decline in Barrick's stock price as I write this. I intend to continue adding incrementally if the downturn in gold prices continues.

Given the dramatic rise in gold prices in the last few months, it naturally causes investors to question whether or not this trend will continue for much longer, or perhaps we may be on the cusp of a multi-year decline like we saw in the aftermath of the last decade-long gold price rally, which ended in 2011. I currently do not see any of the causal factors that are driving gold prices higher dissipating; therefore, I don't think there is a reason to expect a prolonged selloff in gold. There might be many pullbacks in gold prices along the way, but for now, I expect the overall longer-term trajectory to be up.

Assuming that gold prices will continue to rise, I see the possibility of Barrick's stock price reaching new all-time highs next year. The higher price of gold will lead to a continuation of improving financial results in the coming quarters, which will lead to Barrick further consolidating its financial position while also continuing to reward investors in the form of share buybacks and perhaps an increase in the dividend down the line. We should also keep in mind that the market might eventually start to price it as a growth stock, like Cameco.

  • Risks to my thesis.

The most obvious risk factor to watch is the possibility that we will see a prolonged decline in gold prices from here. While I find this to be improbable, it is not impossible. Perhaps a major holder, such as Russia, might become a net seller of gold to shore up its fiscal position due to continued pressures related to the war in Ukraine. Other countries with sizable gold holdings may also be tempted to tap their holdings to deal with fiscal or monetary issues.

The example of Barrick's problems with its mine in Mali is an example of geopolitical risks associated with a company with a significant international footprint. The Reko Diq mine in Pakistan, for instance, is another clear example where internal or international tensions may cause Barrick to lose the mine or have its work suspended for a prolonged period. Just in the past few months, Pakistan engaged in a border clash with India and with Afghanistan, not to mention that it is dealing with internal conflicts as well, with tribal rivalries and so on. Barrick's constant exposure to geopolitics and unstable situations in the countries it operates in should be priced in as a risk factor when one contemplates investing in this company.

  • Miners may outperform the price of gold going forward.

Gold prices are up about 58% YTD, while Barrick's share price has more than doubled so far this year. It is, in my view, a reflection of the fact that the gap between average gold mining costs and the price of gold is getting much wider, leading to an outsized improvement in mining profitability. I see miners continuing to outperform; in other words, if the price of gold goes up 10%, the share price of Barrick can be expected to go up by about 20%. To have Barrick surpass its all-time share price high of 2011, the price of gold would have to appreciate another 40% from here; in other words, reach a level close to $5,500–$6,000/ounce. Given that we just experienced a major pullback in gold prices, this may seem like an improbable outcome, with the market starting to get nervous about this rally. The way I see it, given the global financial situation and other factors, such as central banks showing more willingness to tolerate higher inflation in favor of keeping interest rates lower, I believe that we may see gold prices rise to those levels within a year; therefore, my outlook for Barrick's share price is that it could see new all-time highs within a year.

data.title: NuScale: Danger Signs On Meltdown Of Speculative Froth
data.description:
NuScale: Speculative Take On Nuclear Power?

I've never been that interested in investing in energy companies, let alone companies that specialize in nuclear energy technology like NuScale Power Corporation (NYSE:SMR). However, 2025 has been a real eye-opener even to a seasoned investor and market watcher that I consider myself to be, as the stock of money-losing companies has continued its rampaging run. That said, I'm not particularly perturbed about missing "big speculative opportunities" if they aren't backed by solid fundamentals.

Also, I'm not especially keen on scooping up so-called "undervalued" plays in the fundamentally/structurally challenged sectors of the market (like the oil and gas peers, some healthcare plays, etc.). Investors who position the majority of their portfolios in these sectors have arguably missed out on partaking in the advance impelled by the tech and Mag 7 stocks as they demonstrate their AI prowess while also continuing to build their clout in this fourth industrial AI revolution.

I brought up the issue of speculative opportunities earlier because I believe SMR stock falls right into such a setup. It's not a coincidence that SMR is also the acronym for "small modular reactor" as much as it refers specifically to the ticker symbol for NuScale Power. What's critical for investors to note is that the company has consistently positioned itself as a key technology enabler or provider, encompassing its burgeoning partnership with ENTRA1, while also relying on Doosan as its key manufacturing partner. The reportedly asset-light approach is designed to help NuScale potentially avoid the CapEx complexities and financing challenges entailed with being a developer while also benefiting from the manufacturing expertise of a trusted supply chain partner.

Yet, the drawback suggests that it's imperative for NuScale to manage the supply chain dynamics extremely well in this early stage of the SMR buildout, even though it has obtained NRC approval. In addition, I believe we must also be cognizant of the challenges that encompass the multi-year rollout of NuScale's ability to scale, given the investments it has made in long-lead materials this year to pave the runway for a 2030 commercial operation date status, which is pivotal to justifying the extremely frothy valuation metrics that complement its investment thesis right now.

NuScale: Wall Street Baking In High Expectations

NuScale bulls will likely refer us to the highly optimistic outlook that Wall Street has penciled in on SMR's revenue accretion through the end of the decade. Hence, it's crystal clear that Wall Street is in sync with the commercialization timeline promulgated, although questions in relation to high execution risks, competitiveness, and whether the market share capture reflected in SMR's valuation are appropriate.

Yet, I think with the multi-year unprecedented AI/data center buildout, the need for manufacturing sovereignty, and with renewable energy not assessed to be sufficient to help America meet the full demand spectrum, there's a high sense of urgency to help pave the way for NuScale Power to succeed.

Of course, it remains to be seen whether its technology can realistically lower the costs enough to help sustain its first-mover advantage from approval to production. The pressing need to bolster the energy generation needs to help America unleash the full extent of its AI compute ramp-up to maintain AI primacy cannot be further overstated.

Therefore, NuScale's success isn't just pivotal for shareholders but also crucial to fulfilling the nation's full potential. Notably, China is also expected to become the leading nuclear energy player through the end of the decade, predicated on its overt government support (regulatory, financing, etc.) tech advancements, manufacturing proficiency, and a robust supply chain to anchor its leadership. Therefore, I believe the brewing growth prospects for the SMR value chain have a structurally bullish narrative that shouldn't be dismissed so quickly, even though it's too nascent in the current buildout for us to determine the likely outcome or ultimate winner, making any bet on SMR still a highly speculative one, to say the least.

Buy Or Sell SMR Stock?

Clearly, bulls have dominated the directional bias for SMR since the lows in early 2024. In other words, investors have already been riding the advance for almost two years now, as they bet on the future success of its tech (the market is always forward-looking).

Yet, the stock also met stiff resistance in July 2025 at just under the $53 level. Notably, buyers haven't been able to muster enough momentum to break out of that zone since then, with selling pressure again re-emerging in September and October, aligning with the nearly 27% short interest ratio on SMR stock.

Is the market implying that it doesn't quite concur with the frothy valuations assigned to SMR right now? Consider that the stock is valued at a forward revenue multiple of >66x, while NuScale is only expected to surpass $1 billion in revenue by 2029. Hence, I don't think there's any doubt that the market is baking in a lot of optimism on the potential success of NuScale over the rest of the decade.

But, given that the tech is still nascent, we must be circumspect about whether such a first-mover advantage could plausibly lead to a highly successful commercialization phase, accompanying considerable market share gains for NuScale that are implied in its valuation.

Also, I think we mustn't understate the obvious selling pressure that has hampered buying advances over the $53 level for the past few months, augmenting the narrative that sellers may have loaded up on their bearish positions.

A "Sell" rating appears to be premature for now. Investors should allow more time for us to synthesize the market tape and assess whether the fundamental drivers align with the market adoption that's crucial for NuScale to scale its partnership-heavy model. Until then, I urge you to consider staying on the sidelines, but watch this space carefully.

Rating: "Hold."
